Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I had to work around a lot of other things in my life. I tried to dedicate about 30 hours a week to studying, but it wasn't always at the same times. I'd usually read through materials and take notes late at night when things were quieter. With limited resources, I had to be strategic about when I could access the internet and download what I needed for offline study.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I couldn't afford the luxury of a perfect schedule because sometimes I had to prioritize other responsibilities. When deadlines approached, I'd cram in more hours, but during slower weeks, I might spread things out more. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew something was due soon or when I needed to download materials for later. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline and that would push me to check in. I also logged in to see if there were any peer interactions or messages from classmates since the peer support helped me stay connected. Honestly, necessity drove most of my logins rather than habit.
Q4: There were stretches where logging in became more routine, especially when I got into a rhythm with the online courses. It happened mostly when I found a consistent time slot where I had reliable internet access. The peer interactions helped too because knowing others might respond to my posts gave me a reason to check back. But it was fragile—any disruption to my schedule would break that routine quickly.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful for me because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and articles and study them offline when I didn't have internet. The peer interaction features were also valuable because connecting with other students helped me feel less alone in this. Videos were okay but harder to use with my limited data and unreliable connection.
Q6: I often minimized my use of video content because streaming used too much data and my connection wasn't always stable. Discussion boards I participated in minimally—just one post—because I felt self-conscious and didn't always have the energy to engage deeply. Some optional readings I skipped when I was overwhelmed, figuring I'd come back to them but often didn't. Time and resources were always the limiting factors.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams. If it helped me understand something I was struggling with, that made it valuable. I also appreciated anything I could save and use offline later. When something felt like busywork with no clear payoff, I'd deprioritize it because I couldn't afford to waste limited study time.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were the first things I'd cut when I was stressed or running low on time. Required activities had clear consequences attached, so I made sure to complete those even when I was exhausted. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe later' but later rarely came. There was always this guilt about not doing more, but I had to be realistic about my capacity.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline and wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. I'd download readings, lecture notes, and any materials I could save, then study them on my own time without internet. I also used textbooks from the library and handwritten notes extensively. The LMS was more like a pickup point for materials rather than where I actually did my learning.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd spend hours going through printed materials or PDFs I'd saved, working through problems in notebooks. None of that showed up online. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate in person, quizzing each other on content, and that was some of my most productive learning. The system only saw me when I was downloading or submitting, not when I was actually wrestling with the material.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd log in, download everything I could in one session, then work through it over the following days offline. A single video might get watched once online, but I'd take detailed notes and review those notes multiple times afterward. My actual engagement with the content far exceeded what any login data would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt harder, especially when sadness crept in and motivation dropped. Sometimes financial stress or exhaustion from other responsibilities made it tough to focus on coursework. My attendance was pretty low because I couldn't always make it to things in real-time. During those harder moments, the peer support intervention helped—knowing others were going through similar struggles made me push through.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed by competing responsibilities, my LMS usage became very transactional—just grabbing what I needed and getting out. Confusion about course content sometimes made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face material I didn't understand. The workload felt heavy at times, and I had to triage what got my attention. My medium stress level was constant background noise affecting everything.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I'd have the content open but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about other things or just too tired to focus. Sometimes I'd watch a video but realize at the end I couldn't remember what it covered. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I wasn't confident about the material. The anxiety of potentially failing made me avoid starting sometimes, which I know is backwards. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but that often just compressed everything into last-minute rushes. My assignment scores suffered because of this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the engine that drove my LMS activity. Without a deadline approaching, I might not log in for days. When something was due, suddenly I'd be very active—downloading materials, checking requirements, submitting work. It wasn't healthy, but the deadline pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my low motivation and sadness to get me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback motivated me to engage more with similar content, but critical feedback sometimes made me want to retreat. When I got my exam scores back and saw I was doing okay, it gave me a small boost. But honestly, feedback often came too late to change how I approached the next thing, and sometimes I avoided checking it because I was afraid of disappointment.
Q18: The course structure with 16 online modules gave me flexibility, which I needed given my situation, but it also meant I had to be self-directed when my motivation was low. The lack of rigid scheduling meant I could work around my other responsibilities, but it also made it easy to fall behind. Having clear milestones helped, but I would have benefited from more built-in accountability.
Q19: The flexibility of online access made engagement possible for someone in my situation with limited resources. But the sheer volume of content across 16 courses was overwhelming at times. When materials were well-organized and downloadable, that made things easier. When things required constant online presence or synchronous participation, that was much harder for me to manage.
Q20: I think that pattern might happen when someone is trying to figure out what they need to do but struggling to actually complete the work. Maybe they're confused about requirements or overwhelmed by the content. It could also be someone dealing with personal issues who keeps showing up hoping to engage but can't follow through. Activity doesn't equal productivity when you're struggling.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I performed reasonably well—got a Merit—despite relatively low attendance and not being super visible in discussions. A lot of my learning happened offline where no one could track it. Someone might look at my login frequency and think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in 30 hours a week, just mostly away from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people access materials and submit work, which says something about time management and deadline response. But they miss all the offline studying, the mental effort, the emotional struggles behind the numbers. They can't capture whether someone actually understood what they read or just skimmed it. The human experience of learning is way more complex than clicks and timestamps.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing requirements. It's when you find yourself thinking about course concepts outside of study time, making connections to other things. For me, engagement also meant reaching out to peers when I was struggling and pushing through even when I felt sad or unmotivated. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time spent.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me—limited resources, low motivation, that persistent sadness. It wouldn't capture the hours spent with printed notes or the peer conversations that kept me going. It can't measure resilience or the effort it takes someone in my situation to earn a Merit when the odds aren't in their favor. My journey was harder than my data suggests.
